content:
# Mark Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 9 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 9:2 — "transfigured" vs. "transformed" for μετεμορφώθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-was-transfigured before them" | Traditional; preserves the Latinized theological term. |
| LOGOS | "he was transfigured before them" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "right before their eyes, he was transformed" | Dynamic; "transformed" as more accessible; adds "right before their eyes." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he was transfigured before them" — "transfigured" is the established theological term for this event (μεταμορφόω) and distinguishes this divine transformation from ordinary change.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 9:3 — "fuller" vs. "bleach" for γναφεύς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-fuller on the earth is not able thus to-whiten" | Literal; preserves the trade term. |
| LOGOS | "no fuller on earth could whiten them" | Formal; retains "fuller." |
| PNEUMA | "whiter than any bleach on earth could make them" | Dynamic; replaces the artisan with the product. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "whiter than any fuller on earth could bleach them" — retains the human artisan (γναφεύς) which is Mark's earthly comparison for a heavenly event, while using "bleach" as a verb for naturalness.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended rendering.

---

## 9:5 — "tents" vs. "shelters" for σκηνάς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "three tents" | Literal; σκηνή = tent/tabernacle. |
| LOGOS | "three tents" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "three shelters" | Dynamic; more general term. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "three shelters" — σκηνή in this context refers to the temporary booths of the Festival of Tabernacles (Sukkot). "Shelters" better captures the festival connection than "tents," which evokes camping.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA.

---

## 9:7 — "my beloved Son" vs. "my Son, whom I love" for ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This is my Son, the beloved." | Literal; articular adjective. |
| LOGOS | "This is my beloved Son." | Traditional English rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "This is my Son, whom I love." | Dynamic; relative clause makes the love active. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "This is my beloved Son" — the traditional rendering is both accurate and immediately recognizable as the baptismal/transfiguration formula.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:12 — "restore all things" vs. "set everything right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Elijah indeed, having-come first, restores all-things" | Literal; ἀποκαθιστάνει πάντα. |
| LOGOS | "Elijah does come first to restore all things" | Formal. |
| PNEUMA | "Elijah does come first to set everything right" | Dynamic; "set right" interprets ἀποκαθιστάνω. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "restore all things" — ἀποκαθίστημι means to restore to a former state, and "restore all things" carries the prophetic weight of Malachi 4:5-6.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 9:17–18 — "speechless spirit" / "mute spirit" / "a spirit that has taken away his ability to speak"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having a-speechless spirit" | Literal; πνεῦμα ἄλαλον. |
| LOGOS | "he has a spirit that makes him mute" | Formal; clarifies the causation. |
| PNEUMA | "He's possessed by a spirit that has taken away his ability to speak" | Dynamic; expanded explanation. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He has a spirit that makes him unable to speak" — captures the causal relationship (the spirit renders the boy speechless) while staying close to the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended LOGOS/SOPHIA.

---

## 9:19 — "O faithless generation" vs. "O unbelieving generation" vs. "You faithless generation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "O unbelieving generation" | ἄπιστος = unbelieving. |
| LOGOS | "O faithless generation" | Traditional rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "You faithless generation!" | Direct address; drops vocative "O." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "O faithless generation" — the vocative "O" (ὦ) preserves the lament quality of Jesus's exclamation. "Faithless" captures ἄπιστος well.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:23 — Rendering of τὸ εἰ δύνῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The 'if you-are-able' — all-things [are] possible to-the-one believing." | Preserves the article-plus-quotation structure. |
| LOGOS | "'If you can'! All things are possible for the one who believes." | Exclamation mark; echoing the father. |
| PNEUMA | "'If you can'? Everything is possible for the one who believes." | Question mark; echoing with challenge. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "'If you can'! All things are possible for the one who believes." — the exclamation mark (LOGOS) better captures Jesus's emphatic challenge than the question mark. Jesus is not asking; he is rebuking the conditional.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:24 — "help my unbelief" vs. "help me overcome my doubt"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-believe! Help my unbelief!" | Literal; preserves the paradox. |
| LOGOS | "I believe; help my unbelief!" | Same; semicolon for balance. |
| PNEUMA | "I do believe—help me overcome my doubt!" | Dynamic; "overcome my doubt" interprets ἀπιστία. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "I believe; help my unbelief!" — this is one of the most quoted verses in the Gospels. The paradox of "help my unbelief" is its power. PNEUMA's "overcome my doubt" resolves the paradox too neatly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 9:34 — "who was the greatest" vs. "which of them was the greatest"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who [was] greatest" | Literal; brief. |
| LOGOS | "who was the greatest" | Formal. |
| PNEUMA | "which of them was the greatest" | Clarifies the competitive nature. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who was the greatest" — the simple formulation is faithful to the Greek τίς μείζων, and the context already makes clear they were comparing themselves.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:35 — "he must be last" vs. "he-will-be last" vs. "must be the very last"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-will-be last of-all and servant of-all" | Future tense; ἔσται as prediction. |
| LOGOS | "he must be last of all and servant of all" | Imperative force. |
| PNEUMA | "Whoever wants to be first must be the very last—and the servant of everyone." | Dynamic; "the very last" intensifies; "everyone" for πάντων. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he must be last of all and servant of all" — the future tense ἔσται here functions as a prescriptive statement, and "must be" captures this better than a simple future.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:38 — "we tried to stop him" vs. "we-were-hindering him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we-were-hindering him, because he-was not following us" | Literal; imperfect tense. |
| LOGOS | "we tried to stop him because he was not following us" | Formal; natural English. |
| PNEUMA | "we tried to stop him because he's not one of us" | Dynamic; "not one of us" interprets "not following us." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "we tried to stop him because he was not following us" — "following us" preserves the discipleship vocabulary (ἀκολουθέω) that permeates Mark. PNEUMA's "not one of us" loses this.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:41 — "because you belong to Christ" vs. "in [the] name that you-are of-Christ" vs. "because you belong to the Messiah"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in [the] name that you-are of-Christ" | Literal; preserves the awkward Greek construction. |
| LOGOS | "because you belong to Christ" | Interprets the Greek clause. |
| PNEUMA | "because you belong to the Messiah" | Same interpretation; "Messiah" for Χριστός. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "because you belong to Christ" — the Greek ὅτι Χριστοῦ ἐστε means "because you are Christ's," and LOGOS's rendering is both accurate and natural.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:42 — "causes to stumble" vs. "leads into sin" for σκανδαλίσῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "causes-to-stumble" | Literal; preserves σκανδαλίζω. |
| LOGOS | "causes...to stumble" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "leads one of these trusting little ones into sin" | Dynamic; "leads into sin" interprets; "trusting" for πιστευόντων. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "causes one of these little ones who believe to stumble" — "stumble" preserves the σκανδαλίζω metaphor. "Trusting" (PNEUMA) is a reasonable rendering of πιστευόντων but differs from the established "believe" vocabulary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 9:43 — "Gehenna" vs. "hell" — terminology

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Gehenna" | Preserves the specific geographical/theological term. |
| LOGOS | "Gehenna" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "Gehenna" | Same; consistent across all three. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three agree on "Gehenna" — the proper name is preserved rather than the generic "hell."

**Status:** No disagreement.

---

## 9:50 — "loses its saltiness" vs. "becomes unsalty" vs. "loses its flavor"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if the salt becomes unsalty" | Literal; ἄναλον γένηται. |
| LOGOS | "if the salt loses its saltiness" | Formal; standard rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "if salt loses its flavor" | Dynamic; "flavor" for the salt quality. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "if the salt loses its saltiness" — preserves the wordplay on salt/salty (ἅλας/ἄναλον) better than "flavor," which shifts the metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional theological vocabulary** (transfigured, faithless, Gehenna, Christ) | vv. 2, 19, 41, 43 | Established terms retained |
| **Paradox preservation** ("help my unbelief") | v. 24 | Paradox maintained; dynamic resolution rejected |
| **Rhetorical punctuation** ("If you can" — ! vs. ?) | v. 23 | Exclamation as challenge preferred |
| **Tabernacle/festival terminology** (tents vs. shelters) | v. 5 | Context-sensitive; "shelters" for Sukkot connection |
| **PNEUMA's interpretive expansions** | vv. 3, 17, 24, 38 | Generally not adopted |
| **Discipleship vocabulary** (follow, believe, stumble) | vv. 38, 42 | Consistent with Mark's pattern |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 9 is a chapter of extremes — the Transfiguration's glory, the father's agonized prayer, the disciples' obtuse ambition, and the terrifying Gehenna warnings. The most significant disagreement is the handling of the father's cry in v. 24, where PNEUMA's "help me overcome my doubt" resolves a paradox that should remain unresolved. The three-way consistency on Gehenna (all retain the proper name) and on the bracketed vv. 44, 46 (all bracket them) shows strong textual agreement. The chapter demonstrates that PNEUMA's dynamic approach is most valuable when the Greek is genuinely opaque (v. 5, where "shelters" serves the Sukkot connection better than "tents") but least helpful when it smooths out deliberate textual roughness.
